behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the real filesystem watcher recursion behavior (root vs nested paths), which is OS/watcher-backend dependent. MOCK-MISS: this is not just local parsing logic. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both apply: an in-process fake watcher can report subdirectory events even when the real watcher configuration/backend misses them. Rejection gate: Integration was considered, but rejected because the behavior text requires validating what happens when a file is physically placed in a watched directory tree.
behaviors.test_description: Launch the daemon as a real subprocess against temporary input/output directories. Create `input/subdir/`, then write a file into that subdirectory. Assert exactly one transformed output is produced and corresponds to that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: The key failure is module handoff between request ingestion, dedup state, clock/window config, and pipeline dispatch. MOCK-MISS: this is beyond a single pure function if dedup is enforced at service boundaries. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real OS/network/subprocess required if requests are injected in-process; (b) no AI/ML output dependency; (c) no multi-agent chain. Rejection gate: System was considered, but rejected because the behavior text is specifically about deduping repeated processing requests by path and window, which can be exercised through in-process wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Construct the daemon processing components in-process with a controllable clock (or short real window), and a spy/stub transformer. Inject two processing requests for the same normalized path within the configured window; verify only one dispatch occurs. Then inject again after the window and verify a second dispatch is allowed.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly depends on real OS event duplication patterns from file watchers. REAL-INFRA is mandatory, and MOCK-HIDE applies because mocks can force clean or contrived event sequences that miss backend-specific duplicates/coalescing. Rejection gate: Integration was considered, but rejected by the behavior text referencing OS-emitted multiple filesystem events.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon as a subprocess watching a temp directory. Perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write + rename or chunked writes) and capture processing count for that file. Assert exactly one processing result/output is produced.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS-level watcher lifecycle/rebind scenario (directory deletion/recreation invalidating watch handles). REAL-INFRA is required; in-process fakes often do not reproduce handle invalidation semantics. Rejection gate: Integration was considered, but rejected because the behavior explicitly requires live reaction to real directory removal and recreation while running.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon subprocess watching a temp input dir. Remove the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file. Assert daemon stays alive and processes the post-recreation file without restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness depends on interaction between watcher arrival bursts and real filesystem timestamp metadata/resolution. MOCK-MISS applies at module boundaries (watcher -> metadata -> queue ordering), and MOCK-HIDE applies because synthetic timestamps/events can miss platform quirks. Rejection gate: Integration was considered, but rejected because the behavior text ties ordering to actual file creation timestamps under simultaneous arrival.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon subprocess with temp dirs. Create multiple files in rapid succession/concurrently, record expected order by filesystem creation timestamp as observed by the daemon’s metadata source, then assert processing/output order matches that timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real partial-write timing and OS event timing during ongoing writes. REAL-INFRA is required; fake events or in-memory files cannot reliably reproduce write-in-progress behavior. MOCK-HIDE applies strongly because stubs usually present fully-written files immediately. Rejection gate: Integration was considered, but rejected because the behavior explicitly depends on arrival events firing before write completion on a real filesystem.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon subprocess. Begin writing a large file in chunks with delays (stream kept open) so arrival events fire early. Assert no processing/output occurs before stream close; then assert processing occurs after completion and only once.
